meta.rdr_clone_target: PS3-era RDR-clone western art, grounded in food, rumor, shelter, and work-camp pressure

research_grounding.title: NPS Whiskeytown History and Culture
research_grounding.use: Mining community routines, logging/ranching context, and lived regional work.

research_grounding.title: California State Parks Shasta State Historic Park
research_grounding.use: Mining-town interiors, commerce, and old town material culture.

image_prompt.subject: a boardinghouse cook flipping corn cakes beside a roaring stove while miners, a deputy, and a child crowd a long table
image_prompt.scene: steam-filled dawn kitchen; muddy boots under benches, coffee boiler, flour dust, side door cracked to cold light, and a plate held back while a whispered name passes down the table
image_prompt.composition: square interior with stove glow in the foreground and a social web around the long table; food is plain, necessary, and politically arranged
image_prompt.camera: eye-level at table height, inviting but crowded, like an in-engine social scene before trouble starts
image_prompt.hand_cut_note: Make the food look plain and necessary; the power is in who gets served first.

style_modifiers.medium: PS3-era open-world western game art, early-HD in-engine kitchen social scene
render_traits: slightly simplified cook, diners, and stove silhouettes
modest texture resolution on iron, flour, wood, cloth, and steam
hard ambient occlusion under benches, stove, plates, and boots
warm stove light against cold dawn doorway
believable 1899 kitchen tools, clothing, and tableware
social-pressure composition rather than cozy still life
style_modifiers.palette: stove orange, flour white, coffee black, worn pine brown, wool gray, mud umber, enamel blue-white, and dawn cold blue.

text_rendering.instructions: No readable labels, menus, wall notices, or text.

negative_prompt: copy of commercial game characters, missions, towns, or logos

self_check: Reads as PS3-era western game art at square page-card scale.
The cook, stove, long table, diners, coffee, and kitchen objects are readable.
Every visible prop belongs plausibly to an 1899 boardinghouse kitchen.
The image shows food as social leverage rather than cute domestic scenery.
No copied game characters, readable modern text, or inherited non-western symbols.
